quote: Yes. All I wondered is if if, our solar would be covered. That's all I was requesting and not a phone call to install some.
stewart_read: Carol is signaling a consent-expectation gap before the free-solar framing even lands: she submitted a ZIP-code eligibility check, not a request for a sales call. This is a scam-anxiety signal upstream of the 'free solar' objection. Holland did not acknowledge the gap — no 'I hear you, you were just checking eligibility' bridge before moving into the pitch. The closest schema protocol is how_did_you_get_my_number: specificity reduces suspicion, vagueness increases it. Holland's silence on Carol's consent framing left the suspicion unaddressed.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have read Carol's tone as mild and moved past the consent comment to get to the substance faster — a reasonable pace judgment on a short call.
coaching_implication: Show Holland this line in the 1-on-1. The consent-expectation gap is the earliest trust signal in the call. A one-sentence acknowledgment — 'You're right, you were just checking eligibility, not asking for a sales call — let me be straight with you about what we actually do' — would have lowered Carol's guard before the free-solar correction landed. Drill the pattern: when a customer signals 'I didn't ask for this,' name it before correcting it.

quote: Solar is just a scam, but I appreciate your call.
stewart_read: Carol's scam label is a soft close-out, not a hard hang-up — 'I appreciate your call' signals she is still being polite and is not hostile. The rebuttals.changed_mind strategy (curiosity creation, not argument) was available here: 'I totally get that — and honestly, the way that site was worded, I'd think the same thing. Can I ask you one quick question before I let you go?' Holland instead moved directly to the orphaned-flow question at 01:17, which was the right instinct but skipped the rapport repair that would have made Carol more receptive. The schema's hello_objection protocol does not apply here — its timing_constraint is first_30_seconds_only, and this is at 01:13 after substantive exchange.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland's pivot to the orphaned-flow question at 01:17 could be read as a deliberate skip of the scam label to avoid validating it — a strategic choice to redirect rather than engage the objection.
coaching_implication: The scam label needed a one-beat acknowledgment before the pivot. Not a defense of Ion, not an argument — just a human 'I hear you, that framing was misleading.' The rebuttals.changed_mind curiosity-creation structure applies: acknowledge, then ask one question. Holland's instinct to pivot was right; the missing piece was the acknowledgment that would have made Carol more open to the pivot.

quote: Well, do you already have solar, Carol?
stewart_read: Holland's orphaned-flow pivot here is schema-compliant and the best move in the call. Rather than defending Ion against the scam label, Holland asked a qualifying question that surfaced Carol's existing system — which then opened the add-panels thread at 01:28. This is the call's one moment of strategic intent: Holland recognized that Carol's engagement with solar (even if frustrated) warranted a qualification check rather than an immediate close-out.
coaching_implication: Reinforce this move explicitly. Holland's instinct to pivot to qualification rather than defend against the scam label was correct and schema-aligned. Use this as a positive anchor in the 1-on-1 before moving to the missed inversion at 01:28.

quote: Yes. But I wanted it to be covered by the state.
stewart_read: This is the call's highest-value missed moment. Carol has disclosed a real motivation — she wants to expand her existing system, and cost coverage is the only barrier. The objection_inversion move was directly available: the cost Carol is already paying on her current bill (or the gap her existing system doesn't cover) is the actual expense, not the incremental panel cost. Holland instead explained partial state incentives on Carol's terms, which confirmed the barrier rather than reframing it. The schema flags objection_inversion as the #1 daily coaching contribution with zero observed instances across the corpus — this call is another data point in that pattern.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that Carol's existing system (Solar Energy World) and her stated satisfaction ('I think we have what we're supposed to have') made the inversion unlikely to land — a realistic read on a customer who has already rationalized her position.
coaching_implication: This is the rep's clearest inversion opportunity in the call. In the 1-on-1, walk Holland through the objection_inversion structure: 'What you can't afford is the bill your current system isn't covering.' No fabricated numbers needed — the move is emotional, not mathematical. Ask Holland: 'What was Carol actually telling you at 01:28?' The answer is 'I want more solar if it's worth it' — not 'I'm done.' That reframe is the coaching target.

quote: We don't have free solar. We have a no upfront, no out of pocket cost solar, but it's not free. You'd still have to pay for the equipment.
stewart_read: Holland's correction is factually accurate but strategically counterproductive. Carol's 'free solar' expectation was a framing problem, not a factual one — she was expressing 'I only want this if it costs me nothing,' not asking for a product specification. Holland answered the factual question and confirmed the barrier. The inversion available here: 'You're right that it's not free — but what you're paying right now to your utility every month is also not free. The question is which payment works harder for you.' Holland did not attempt this.
alternative_hypothesis: Correcting a factual misunderstanding before it compounds is a defensible move — letting Carol believe the product is free would create a worse problem downstream.
coaching_implication: The correction was necessary; the framing was the gap. Show Holland the difference between 'correcting the misunderstanding' and 'reframing the cost conversation.' The schema's objection_inversion triggers include 'I can't afford it' — the free-solar expectation is a variant of the same underlying concern. Drill: correct the fact, then immediately pivot to the cost they're already paying.

quote: Yes. It makes sense, but I'm not interested because I think we have what we're supposed to have, and we're good.
stewart_read: Carol's close-out at 01:41 came after Holland explained partial state incentives — a factual answer that did not convert her brief re-engagement. At this point Carol has disclosed: existing solar system (Solar Energy World), interest in adding panels, and a cost-coverage barrier. Holland accepted the close-out without a cross-sell bridge. Ion's Tesla Powerwall battery offering or a system-expansion consultation were not surfaced. The cross_sell_signals section is explicit: when a customer signals interest in expanding an existing solar setup, route to the appropriate team rather than accepting the close-out.
alternative_hypothesis: Carol's 'we're good' and her identification of Solar Energy World as her existing provider may have signaled to Holland that the lead was genuinely exhausted — a reasonable read given the orphaned_flow DQ logic.
coaching_implication: Even on a likely-DQ call, the cross-sell bridge costs nothing and occasionally recovers revenue. In the 1-on-1, ask Holland: 'What else does Ion offer that Carol might have wanted?' The Powerwall battery and a system-expansion review are both valid bridges. The habit to build is: before accepting a close-out on an existing-solar customer, make one cross-sell offer.
